text: stairs?” Ronin asked . Mercy pointed to the west of the entrance. “Some places are cluttered on the upper floors. Old equipment and furnishings, mostly .” “Go back into the repair room. Should be deep enough inside to be safe from any gunfire .” She nodded, lips dropping into a frown. She touched Ronin’s shoulder lightly. “Be careful. He’s won’t stop until you’re all deactivated, or he’s just a heap of spare parts . Ronin watched her walk down the pristine hallway for several seconds before he returned his attention to Maul. Most of the soldiers had dispersed to more advantageous positions. A small group of them remained, moving the seats and desk to create a makeshift barrier across the entry . The siren blared again . Was Lara all right? He hadn’t heard any other shots; shouldn’t that be of some comfort ? “Where do you want me, Sergeant?” Ronin asked . “A window on one of the upper floors. We’ll hold this room here, but if you’re half as quick as you seem to be, I don’t think any of them will make it this far .” “Here’s hoping .” As Maul and another synth pushed a row of heavy, connected chairs into place, Ronin found the stairs. He took them three at a time, up to the fourth floor . Dozer and two human soldiers — Jensen and Ramirez — were already in position. The humans stood at two narrow windows, with Dozer at the large, circular window in between them. Ronin ran his optics over the objects piled in the room — filing cabinets, chairs, old desks, and cardboard boxes that looked like they would crumble at the slightest touch, all blanketed in a thick layer of dust. Leaning up against some of it was a long, weatherworn sign, words faded but legible . The Price of Freedom is Visible Here . It seemed fitting, but how high would that price be ? He moved to the round window, pressing his shoulder to the wall opposite Dozer. The grounds below remained quiet; grass and trees swayed in the wind, and dawn light steadily crept in from the east. Beyond, the many residences sat peacefully, indifferent to the inevitable conflict that would soon tear Cheyenne apart . From this vantage, the human slums were visible beyond the wall, relatively dark compared to the rest of the town. His hope to spot Lara was a foolish one, but he clung to it regardless. She was out there, recently recovered from serious injuries, risking her life for all the people — organic and metal — in Cheyenne . Most of whom had never done anything for her . “Still doing okay?” he asked Dozer . “As long as we’re done by lunch time, I’ll be fine .” “I’m sorry — ” “No. You’re the reason I don’t have more holes in my casing. I’d look like Swiss cheese otherwise .” “Swiss cheese?” Ronin’s processors poured over decades of data. “I understand that, though I don’t recall having ever seen it .” “Yeah, same here. Weird, the stuff that lingers even after the Memory Wipe. Can’t remember if I had a name, or relationships…or anything else .” “But we hold onto a cheese full of holes,” he said, optics trained on the grounds below. “It’s for the best, most likely. What good would it do for us to remember a dead world? Better to connect to the world we have and build it anew as best we can .” “Old world must not have been too great anyway,” said Jensen, “else it wouldn’t have ended like it did .” “I could imagine a few better things than living and dying in a concrete hole,” Ramirez replied. “Maybe one of those beaches, like in Anderson’s old vids .” “Guess there used to be some pretty tall buildings, too. Like, even taller than this one.” Jensen’s skepticism was clear in his voice . “Everything was bigger, back then,” Dozer said, staring out at the grounds . “Big enough to make this mess when it all came down.” A twitch crackled over Ronin’s cheek, but he ignored it. The old world had exploded, and this one was harsh and unforgiving, but he had Lara here. He didn’t need skyscrapers or beaches or automobiles or holey cheese; she was all he required in this world or any other . The chain of probabilities — the ‘what-ifs’ — spiraled on into infinity, daunting even to his processors. So many things could have gone differently, so many variables might have resulted in a world without his fiery Lara Brooks . His optics detected movement on the road, beyond the trees . Gearheads. Some of their marks were visible through the gaps between the boughs. At least half a dozen of them approaching from the east and three or four more from the west. The two groups combined and crossed the field, heading north — directly toward the front entrance. Due to the trees at the edge of the lawn, Ronin didn’t have a clear line-of-sight, but they would have to break from the cover to cross the circular driveway below the windows . “Get ready, boys,” Dozer said flatly . Ramirez released a shaky breath . The gearheads — ten in total — emerged from the trees and hurried along the cracked pavement, toward the patch of grass at the center of the drive. One hundred and fifty feet…one hundred and forty … They slowed, undoubtedly spotting their deactivated comrades and the barricaded front doors . “Now!” Dozer called . She and Ronin shattered the window and leveled their weapons. He selected a target — the gearhead called Northside — and opened fire. More rifles went off to either side and on the lower levels. Bullets rained down on the gearheads, punching holes in their casings, ricocheting to cut grooves in the grass and dirt. One gearhead went down almost immediately, limbs shaking and bending at
